command.text: find /testbed -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -type f -exec wc -w {} + | awk '{if($1<2000){sum+=$1;cnt++}} END{print cnt?sum/cnt:"0"}'

raw_output_payload.content: find /testbed -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -type f -exec wc -w {} + | awk '{if($1<2000){sum+=$1;cnt++}} END{print cnt?sum/cnt:"0"}'
raw_output_payload.reasoning:
We need bash command to compute mean average of word count of .txt files <2000 words in /testbed. Use wc -w, filter by <2000, sum and count, then average. One-liner. Use find. Example:

find /testbed -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -type f -exec wc -w {} + | awk 'NR>1{sum+=$1;cnt++} END{print sum/cnt}'

But need to exclude files >2000 words. So we need to filter. Use awk to check. Maybe:

find /testbed -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -type f -print0 | xargs -0 wc -w | awk 'NR>1{if($1<2000){sum+=$1;cnt++}} END{print cnt?sum/cnt:"0"}'

But wc -w output includes filename. So first field is word count. Use awk accordingly. Also need to handle header? wc -w with multiple files prints each line and total. But using -w with multiple files prints each line and total. We can avoid total by using -w for each file separately: find ... -exec wc -w {} + gives each line with count and filename, no total. So use that.

Thus command:

find /testbed -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -type f -exec wc -w {} + | awk 'NR>1{if($1<2000){sum+=$1;cnt++}} END{print cnt?sum/cnt:"0"}'

But NR>1? Actually no header. So just process all lines. So:

find /testbed -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -type f -exec wc -w {} + | awk '{if($1<2000){sum+=$1;cnt++}} END{print cnt?sum/cnt:"0"}'

Return average.

find /testbed -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -type f -exec wc -w {} + | awk '{if($1<2000){sum+=$1;cnt++}} END{print cnt?sum/cnt:"0"}'

task.query: Compute the mean average of the word count of *.txt files smaller than 2000 words in the /testbed directory
task.gold: find /testbed  -name '*.txt' -print0 | xargs -0 wc -w | awk '$1 < 2000 {v += $1; c++} END {print v/c}'
